subject: Your AI policy might be solving the wrong problem
previewText: Most districts start with restrictions. The ones making real progress start with purpose.
headline: Fear-first AI policies leave everyone guessing.
subhead: Here's how the best districts reframe the conversation and get better results.
body:
[First name],

Most districts' first conversation about AI started with fear. That's understandable. But policies built around fear tend to produce long lists of restrictions and very little clarity on what good AI use actually looks like.

The result? Teachers and students don't know what they're supposed to do. They just know what they can't.

The districts making the most meaningful progress take a different approach. They start by asking:

  • What learning can AI actually unlock for our students?
  • Where are our current approaches falling short?
  • What does responsible use look like in our specific context?

When the vision is clear, the guardrails make sense. Teachers understand why boundaries exist. Leaders can evaluate new tools against real goals, not just headlines.

Risk still matters. Privacy, safety, and academic integrity are essential. But purpose has to come first.

If your district is ready to move from reactive to proactive, or if you have a policy that started with fear and want to rebalance it, we'd love to help.


Dr. Rob Wessman
VP of Ethics, Safety & Learning Innovation, SchoolAI
